name: Hiền Lương Bridge – 17th Parallel – Historic Hiền Lương Bridge

reviews.author: Kevin G
reviews.text: The Hiền Lương Bridge, built in 1928 and reconstructed in 1950, became a powerful symbol of Vietnam’s division at the 17th Parallel during the Vietnam War. For over 20 years, it marked the boundary between North and South Vietnam until reunification in 1975. 🏗 Origins and Construction • 1928: Originally built by local residents of Vinh Linh District as a simple wooden bridge, only 2 meters wide, supported by iron poles, and used mainly by pedestrians. • 1950: The French rebuilt it as a concrete bridge, 162 meters long and 3.6 meters wide, capable of supporting military vehicles up to 10 tons. 🌍 Strategic Location • The bridge spans the Bến Hải River in Quảng Trị Province, right along the 17th Parallel, which was designated as the temporary military demarcation line under the 1954 Geneva Accords. • This line divided Vietnam into two zones: North Vietnam (Democratic Republic of Vietnam) and South Vietnam (Republic of Vietnam) The symbolic importance of the Hiền Lương Bridge at the 17th Parallel, which served as a crossing point and a powerful reminder of Vietnam’s division and eventual reunification. Unfortunately nothing left of the original bridge, when I was there they were re- building it.

reviews.author: Trang Ngô
reviews.text: With 2 colors (blue for Northern Vietnam, yellow for Southern in Vietnam war), this bridge was considered the border between North and South Vietnam after the 1954 Geneva Agreement (aka the 17th parallel) Come here to breathe in the history of the fallen, the heroic, the unforgetable. 🇻🇳🍀

reviews.author: Vincent jeffs
reviews.text: Currently under refurbishment construction so you can’t walk across but bridge and parks surrounding will be nice once completed.

reviews.author: Luke Ismay
reviews.text: The bridge has been destroyed. They are currently constructing a new bridge. The old archway from north to south is the only remnant.

reviews.author: Adrian M
reviews.text: The old bridge separating the North from the South until after the war. It remains as a historical site for tourism, since there's a more modern bridge in use next to it. It's nice to walk along and between the line on the bridge, imagining what used to be.

reviews.author: Xiu Be
reviews.text: The bridge crossing Bến Hải river with demarcation of the separation of the North & South VN during war time in the DMZ. Walking on it, I get a feeling of time travel back some 60 years.

reviews.author: Nguyễn Trang
reviews.text: Great place. Beautiful monuments about the splitting of family with 17 parallel. The wife waited his communist husband 20 year to reunite since each were in 2 sides of 17 parallel

reviews.author: Roger Huntjens
reviews.text: Bridge connecting north and south in the middel of the Demilitarised Zone (DMZ). It’s alongside highway 1 (also known as Highway 1, m=Vietnamese: Đại lộ Kinh Hoàng=. But is got famous as Highway of Horror, because of the killing of many South Vietnamese soldiers and civilians by the People's Army of Vietnam (PAVN) during the Easter Offensive of the Vietnam War, on Highway 1, between Quảng Trị and Huế, South Vietnam from 30 March to 2 May 1972. The 1st monument on the north represents the man who are longing to get back to their families in the south. The 2nd monument on the south represents their families longing for their husbands/fathers to get home safe. The last picture is from the bridge between them which is painted blue on the north (original colour from the south, adopted to show unification which first was communist red) and yellow from the south. Both exactly until the middle of the bridge.

reviews.author: Quy Nguyen
reviews.text: One of the most interesting place to visit. You will see the fact of the war during the visit in the display house. Both sides also built massive flag poles in a race of size and height, ending with a 38.6 metre height pole built by the North. After this, the South attempted to destroy the opposing flag by intense airstrikes and naval bombardment. Later during the 1965-68 American bombardment campaign, the DRV flags was also targeted many times by American airstrikes. The 1967 bombing destroyed both the bridge and the flag pole. DRV responded by rebuilt a new temporary flag pole every time the old one was destroyed. Sewing machines were sent to the frontline to reduce the required manufacture time. Local people also assisted the sewing, with two women named Trần Thị Viễn and Ngô Thị Diễm was awarded the title of Hero for their significant contribution to the task.

reviews.author: Justin P
reviews.text: An important piece of landmark history that divided the north and south back during the war. Great little exploration if you're into this kind of history. The weather is hot during mid August so not for the faint hearted if you can't handle the heat as there's minimum to no shade walking around. The bridge that connects the two lands back then is still standing but is a bit worn out however you can easily take a car or bike on the newly built road bridge to cross between the two lands without paying for entry fee. Otherwise worth it for the 50k VND to see what the area represents by foot. There's a small museum as well that shows some graphical images of the past so again. Not for the faint hearted.

reviews.author: Adam Yan
reviews.text: I’ve been waiting for over 4 years and finally I am here! The history behind it is fascinating and definitely worth visiting for both Vietnamese and foreigners!

reviews.author: Michael Matus
reviews.text: At the 17th parallel. Very interesting if you are interested in the America vs. Vietnam war.

reviews.author: Pradeep Hewavitharana
reviews.text: Wheelchair-friendly place. But no access to the museum or old bridges. A significant place to visit. No toilet facilities for the disabled.

reviews.author: Kerry Boettcher
reviews.text: Very interesting especially the bridge. Grounds not maintained though..

reviews.author: Leon Voskov
reviews.text: Definitely stop if traveling south to hue. The history museum had some great items.

reviews.author: Huyen Tran
reviews.text: Another landmark in Quang Tri. This bridge was separated Vietnam to the north and south back to the old day.

reviews.author: Gideon Meyer
reviews.text: Historical place and therefore b important to see, but nothing special.

reviews.author: Tristan Passeggiati
reviews.text: A really interesting historical spot, but be aware that you have to pay to walk over the bridge (50,000 VND when we went) the north side is well maintained, the south side is not. Really sobering to get to interact with such history between our two countries.

reviews.author: Des P
reviews.text: Wonderful to see - worth the visit

reviews.author: Charlie Foulger
reviews.text: A beautiful piece of history. It's been persevered with the differing colours on the bridge and really brings the history of the division to life.

reviews.author: Thang Nguyen
reviews.text: This is a famous historical relic of Vietnam. This place has witnessed many separations and reunions of ethnic people in the resistance war against the US. Currently the area is being given to tourists. Here, tourists will have to pay an entrance fee of 50,000 VND. The main impression is that the flagpole is more than 15m high, here it is possible to take souvenir photos. Inside, there is a wooden building, only displaying a few historical photos, some statues depicting the old activities of the staff here. All of them are old and dirty, you can see spider net on statues. No landscape inside, grass and bushes everywhere. No one takes care! I wonder what do they do with entrance fee? What do they Treat with history location?

reviews.author: Lauren Schneider
reviews.text: The river separated North Vietnam and South Vietnam for many years. The bridge is half yellow and half blue, to the middle, representing the two sides which had checkpoints to search people crossing. Interesting monuments on both sides of the river and a small museum; a comfortable walk between them all. Definitely worth visiting but you will get the most out of the trip if you go with a guide to hear about the history, as we did, or someone who reads Vietnamese and can translate the information on plaques and murals for you.

reviews.author: Robert Hoàng
reviews.text: Back to the old Battlefield to learn more about what happened in Vietnam War

reviews.author: Tuan Tran
reviews.text: History place, separate Vietnam to 2 country, North and south.

reviews.author: Thuy Nguyen
reviews.text: This historic place recalls a lot about Vietnam history, when the country was divided into two regions: North and South. Many people died at this place, on both sides of the bridge. Stand here to think about the past and hope no more war in the future. Still very poor area after nearly 45 years the war ended

reviews.author: Olly Pirozek
reviews.text: Good to visit on your way past given it’s history. But it’s quite expensive and the museum is very dated and a lot of the signs are only in Vietnamese. Nice spot but don’t make a special trip.

reviews.author: Gun Ho
reviews.text: Not the original one but worth a visit for people with old memory of Vietnam war.

reviews.author: V. Lucas
reviews.text: 50,000VND to enter the site (same as the Vinh Moc tunnels in comparison), way too much for Vietnam since you can see everything from the outside. Just walk around the bridge etc, without entering and you can get some nice pics of this symbolic place.

reviews.author: Seppe
reviews.text: Beautiful place although 50k for entry was a little bit much since there's not so much too see, there's only the bridge and the monument.
